title: Getting back to your hotel after a long theme-park day
context: It's 10 PM and you're leaving EPCOT or Hollywood Studios alone.
advice: Disney's complimentary buses and Minnie Vans run late, but they fill fast after park close. If you're staying off-property, book an Uber or Lyft from inside the park before you reach the exit — wait times spike the moment the gates open.

title: Navigating International Drive without a rental car
context: Your hotel is on I-Drive and you want to reach downtown or the airport.
advice: The I-Ride Trolley covers the length of I-Drive cheaply, but stops well short of downtown. For anywhere beyond the corridor, Uber and Lyft are the practical choice. SunRail reaches downtown from Kissimmee — useful if you're willing to plan around its schedule.

title: Feeling isolated on a solo trip
context: You're surrounded by families at the parks and the evenings feel quiet.
advice: Switch your venue mix: Universal CityWalk and I-Drive dining are more adult and solo-friendly than Disney resorts. Food tours in the Milk District and the Audubon Park Garden District plug you into a genuinely local crowd.

title: Booking a theme-park day without a group
context: You're unsure whether single-rider lines and solo dining are worth it.
advice: Yes — single-rider queues at Universal can cut wait times by half. For Disney, use the Genie+ and Lightning Lane system early in the morning. Solo dining at quick-service spots is completely normal; table-service reservations for one are easy to grab on short notice.
